summary: Jeff Atwood explores Paul Graham's provocative claim that design patterns indicate language deficiencies, using Lisp as the prime example. The post examines Peter Norvig's finding that most Gang of Four patterns become invisible in Lisp due to its malleability. Atwood engages with a wiki discussion proposing that design patterns are actually how programming languages evolve, similar to how linguistic abbreviations become standard words. While gently mocking Graham's sometimes condescending tone about Lisp superiority, Atwood ultimately agrees that excessive pattern reliance signals language limitations and that languages evolve slowly by absorbing common patterns into native features.
key_points: Peter Norvig found that 16 of 23 Gang of Four design patterns are 'invisible or simpler' in Lisp, suggesting patterns compensate for language limitations
Design patterns may represent the evolutionary mechanism for programming languages, similar to how contractions became standard English words
Even constructs we take for granted today (if-then-else, do-while) were once design patterns that became language features
Lisp's continued relevance despite being one of the oldest languages demonstrates how slow language evolution actually is
The .NET framework's evolution with constructs like 'Using', Nullable, and Generics shows patterns being absorbed into language syntax
money_quotes: When I see patterns in my programs, I consider it a sign of trouble.
The shape of a program should reflect only the problem it needs to solve.
Any other regularity in the code is a sign, to me at least, that I'm using abstractions that aren't powerful enough.
Has anyone ever considered that design patterns are the way that programming languages evolve?
One wonders, then, why Lisp hasn't become sentient and taken over the world by now.
Excessive reliance on design patterns is indicative of failings in the language.
Lisp is so malleable that design patterns immediately become part of the base language; they are indistinguishable from the original core language constructs.
key_insight: Design patterns are not timeless best practices but rather symptoms of language limitations that eventually get absorbed into more expressive languages as native features.
